q: What is an example of a customer pain point in banking and how does a fintech address this?

options.A: Consumers can't open accounts quickly; challenger banks offer 10-minute mobile onboarding.
options.B: Consumers lack budgeting tools; Sensibill white-labels a receipt app to banks.
options.C: Consumers don't know their credit score; Credit Karma and Borrowell give free scores.
options.D: Consumers pay high FX fees; Revolut offers a multi-currency prepaid debit card.

q: How did the 2008-2009 Global Financial Crisis lower the barriers to entry for fintechs into banking?

options.A: It forced banks to raise capital, leaving no funds to compete with fintech start-ups.
options.B: It destroyed the hurdles of trust and regulation, prompting policies encouraging new entrants.
options.C: It eliminated screen scraping, requiring banks to build open APIs for fintechs.
options.D: It made banking highly profitable, attracting fintechs to the industry's profit pools.

q: What is the value proposition of a fintech app developer and their strategy for building and scaling their business?

options.A: An inexpensive solution to one pain point; unbundle a product, then rebundle complementary services.
options.B: A full-service digital bank; bundle third-party products via APIs to create network effects.
options.C: A multi-sided platform charging the money-side commissions on products sold to users.
options.D: A licensed money service business targeting travelers with a no-fee prepaid card.

q: Why do fintechs want to adopt a B2B model and white-label their mobile apps to incumbents?

options.A: B2B lets fintechs avoid open banking regulations that apply only to consumer-facing apps.
options.B: Banks pay higher fees than consumers because they lack any regulatory expertise.
options.C: White-labeling lets fintechs display their own brand inside the bank's mobile app.
options.D: B2C acquisition costs $500-$1,000 per customer, so fintechs license apps to banks with large customer bases.

q: What is a challenger bank and how is it different from a traditional bricks-and-mortar bank?

options.A: A software firm licensing banking apps to incumbents rather than serving customers directly.
options.B: A neobank offering the same products but delivering an entirely end-to-end digital experience.
options.C: A multi-sided marketplace selling third-party banking products without holding a license.
options.D: A data aggregator that connects customer accounts to fintech apps using APIs.

q: How are challenger banks doing around the world and who are some leading players?

options.A: Thriving, with over 300 globally; leaders include Revolut, N26, Nubank, and Chime.
options.B: Declining, with fewer than 50 survivors; leaders include Plaid, Yodlee, and Mint.
options.C: Concentrated only in Europe; leaders include Fidor, Borrowell, and Wealthsimple.
options.D: Struggling to grow, with 300 globally but fastest growth in the UK and Germany.

q: What has been the go-to-market strategy of challenger banks and are they regulated?

options.A: They launch abroad first for scale, and all must hold a full banking license to operate.
options.B: They build multi-sided platforms, and none are regulated since lending is unregulated.
options.C: They build a foothold with one product then rebundle and expand abroad; regulation splits by whether they hold a banking license.
options.D: They partner with incumbents from day one, and only money service business licenses are ever required.

q: What is the business model and monetization strategy of a financial marketplace?

options.A: A one-sided business earning lending income and fees like a challenger bank.
options.B: A centralized agent licensing white-labeled software to banks under a SaaS model.
options.C: A principal holding deposits and underwriting loans while charging monthly subscriptions.
options.D: A multi-sided platform connecting customers to third-party products, earning fees, commissions, and advertising.

q: What are some of the strategies adopted by incumbents in response to fintech new entrants?

options.A: A single mandated strategy: all incumbents must build digital banks in-house.
options.B: A menu based on capabilities and scale: build, R&D, partner, license, invest, acquire, or target fintechs.
options.C: Only defensive lobbying to block open banking and preserve their data moats.
options.D: Exclusively acquiring fintechs, since partnering and licensing always fail from culture clash.

q: What is banking-as-a-service (BaaS) and what are the advantages for both the fintech and the bank partner?

options.A: A licensed bank provides the regulated back office so fintechs offer white-labeled banking products, each focusing on its strengths.
options.B: A fintech provides technology so banks can outsource their customer-facing apps and branding.
options.C: A multi-sided marketplace where banks sell third-party products for fees and commissions.
options.D: An open banking API standard that forces banks to share customer data with any fintech.
